skill.name: python-cli-patterns
skill.description: Python CLI development patterns for Typer, Rich, configuration files, environment variables, command groups, testing, and terminal output.
skill.summary: Build structured Python command-line apps with Typer, Rich, configuration handling, and tests.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static detections for shell execution, weak crypto, network access, dynamic imports, and hidden file access are mostly false positives from Markdown fences, inline backticks, Rich symbols, and documentation examples. No prompt injection, obfuscation, malicious network behavior, or external command execution was found. The remaining risk is instructional: one example executes CLI-provided SQL directly, and several examples handle secret-bearing configuration values.

medium_findings.title: Raw SQL Execution Pattern
medium_findings.description: The Typer context example accepts a SQL string from the command line and passes it directly to db.execute. This is not malicious by itself, but it can teach unsafe query handling if copied into tools that receive untrusted input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data flow is direct: a CLI argument named sql is executed without validation or parameter binding. The risk is contextual because database administration CLIs may intentionally run raw SQL.

low_findings.title: Secret-Bearing Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The configuration documentation demonstrates DB_PASSWORD, DATABASE_URL, and api_key settings. These are legitimate CLI patterns, but generated applications should avoid logging or printing these values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines clearly reference secret-bearing environment variables and settings. I did not find evidence that the skill exfiltrates or prints these secrets.

low_findings.title: Whole Configuration File Display
low_findings.description: The template includes a command that reads and prints the full configuration file. This is useful for debugging, but it may expose secrets if users later store credentials in that file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code reads and displays the entire config file, but the default sample config contains only non-secret server settings. The issue depends on later user configuration.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Analyzer Clusters
low_findings.description: The reported external command, weak crypto, dynamic import, network, and hidden-file findings are false positives. They come from Python code fences, inline Markdown examples, Rich checkmark symbols, normal imports, XDG paths, and sample host values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are documentation examples or normal Python imports and output formatting. I found no subprocess calls, shell evaluation, hashlib md5 or sha1 usage, or outbound network request.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unparameterized SQL Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: A CLI argument is used as the SQL statement passed to the database execution method. Generated tools should restrict this to trusted administrator workflows or use parameterized queries.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example directly connects a command-line string to db.execute. The pattern is visible and does not rely on inferred code outside the file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unredacted Configuration Output
dangerous_patterns.description: A command prints the complete configuration file content. Generated CLIs should redact keys such as password, token, secret, api_key, and database_url before display.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The template prints the loaded config content, but the bundled default config is not secret-bearing. The risk appears when users extend the config with credentials.

content.value_statement: Python CLI projects often need consistent command structure, validation, configuration, and terminal output. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code reusable patterns for Typer, Rich, config files, and tests.
actual_capabilities: Creates Typer command functions with arguments, options, callbacks, and command groups.
Adds Rich tables, panels, progress bars, logging handlers, markdown, syntax highlighting, and live displays.
Shows configuration patterns using TOML files, XDG directories, environment variables, and layered overrides.
Provides a reusable Python CLI template with config subcommands, status output, and version handling.
Demonstrates CLI testing with Typer CliRunner and environment-variable test setup.
limitations: It provides patterns and examples; it does not install dependencies or run project scaffolding by itself.
It focuses on Typer and Rich, so Click or argparse coverage is limited.
Some examples need security hardening before use with untrusted SQL, secrets, or configuration files.
It assumes Python 3.10 or newer and modern typing syntax.

use_cases.title: Create a New CLI Tool
use_cases.description: Generate a Typer application with commands, options, help text, version output, and basic config handling.

use_cases.title: Improve Terminal Output
use_cases.description: Add Rich tables, panels, progress indicators, logging output, and syntax highlighting to an existing command-line app.

use_cases.title: Standardize CLI Configuration
use_cases.description: Design predictable configuration loading from defaults, files, environment variables, and command-line overrides.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Python Typer command for my project. Include typed arguments, useful option names, clear help text, and friendly error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Add Rich Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Update my CLI output with Rich. Use tables for records, progress indicators for long tasks, and clear success and error messages.

prompt_templates.title: Design Configuration Loading
prompt_templates.prompt: Design configuration loading for my Python CLI. Use defaults, a TOML config file, environment variables, and CLI overrides in a clear priority order.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Production CLI
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Python CLI for command structure, Rich output, config handling, tests, secret redaction, unsafe SQL, and maintainability issues.

output_examples.input: Request: Create a CLI with user commands, version output, and readable help.
output_examples.output: A Typer app layout with grouped commands, typed options, docstrings for help text, a version callback, and consistent success and error output.

output_examples.input: Request: Make command output easier to scan in the terminal.
output_examples.output: A Rich-based presentation plan using tables for rows, panels for summaries, progress bars for long tasks, and colored error messages.

output_examples.input: Request: Add configuration support to a command-line app.
output_examples.output: A configuration approach that loads defaults first, then a TOML file, then environment variables, and finally explicit CLI options.

best_practices: Use typed Typer options and arguments with clear help text and validation.
Redact secrets before printing configuration, environment variables, or connection details.
Test commands with CliRunner, including success paths, invalid input, and environment-variable behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not execute raw SQL from command-line input unless the tool is strictly for trusted administrators.
Do not print full configuration files when they may contain passwords, tokens, or database URLs.
Do not add decorative terminal output that makes errors, prompts, or automation logs harder to parse.

faq.question: What Python CLI framework does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Typer for command structure and Rich for terminal output.

faq.question: Can it help with configuration files?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes TOML loading, XDG directories, environment variables, and layered override patterns.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete project?
faq.answer: It includes a reusable CLI template, but it does not provide packaging, dependency management, or release automation.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning. The main concerns are unsafe SQL examples and secret-handling patterns.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No evidence of outbound network behavior was found in the reviewed files.

faq.question: How should users handle secrets with this skill?
faq.answer: Users should load secrets from approved sources and redact them from logs, status commands, and configuration output.
